ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for stir-fried green pepper with garlic; closest_parent is stir-fried vegetable food product (FOODON_03301710).
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers cooked peppers and chillies; no specific code for stir-fried green pepper with garlic preparation.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code identified for this specific stir-fried vegetable dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of stir-fried green pepper with garlic is approximately 150 g, equivalent to one side dish portion as served in a Chinese restaurant or zi char stall.

nutrient_source.title: Stir-fried green pepper (capsicum) with garlic — estimated from USDA FoodData Central raw green bell pepper (FDC ID 170108) and USDA cooking retention factors for stir-frying, with garlic and oil contribution modelled from USDA SR Legacy
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for stir-fried green pepper with garlic in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Values estimated from USDA FDC raw green bell pepper (FDC ID 170108), adjusted for stir-frying water loss (~15%), with garlic (FDC ID 169230) at ~8% weight and vegetable oil (FDC ID 172336) at ~5% weight contribution. Soy sauce sodium contribution modelled at ~3% weight. This is a medium-confidence estimate.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of non-starchy vegetables — green pepper / capsicum analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008) International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for stir-fried green pepper with garlic in the University of Sydney GI Database. Green bell pepper is a non-starchy vegetable with very low digestible carbohydrate content. GI estimated at 15 by analogy with other non-starchy cooked vegetables (GI range 10–20) per Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC (2008) International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008, Diabetes Care 31(12):2281–2283. GL per 150 g serving = (15 × 6.2 × 1.5) / 100 = 1.4.

hypertension.guidance: Sodium is approximately 320 mg per 100 g due to soy sauce used in stir-frying; individuals with hypertension should request reduced soy sauce or low-sodium soy sauce and limit portion size to manage daily sodium intake below 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index and low net carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 15 and only 6.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g, stir-fried green pepper with garlic has a negligible glycemic load and is well-suited for inclusion in a carbohydrate-controlled diet for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat, high vitamin C, and beneficial phytonutrients
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The dish is low in saturated fat (0.6 g/100 g), cholesterol-free, and provides vitamin C and antioxidant compounds from green pepper and garlic that may support endothelial health; however, sodium from soy sauce should be monitored in individuals with concurrent hypertension and CVD.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Stir-fried green pepper with garlic (蒜炒青椒, suàn chǎo qīng jiāo) is a classic Chinese home-style and zi char dish consisting of sliced or julienned green bell peppers (capsicum) rapidly stir-fried in a hot wok with generous amounts of minced or sliced garlic, vegetable oil, soy sauce, and salt. The high-heat wok technique produces a dish with a characteristic smoky aroma (wok hei), tender-crisp pepper texture, and a savoury, mildly pungent garlic flavour. The dish is served as a vegetable side dish alongside steamed rice and other main courses in Chinese family meals, hawker centres, and zi char restaurants across Singapore and China. Nutritionally, it is a low-calorie, low-carbohydrate vegetable dish providing approximately 72 kcal per 100 g, with meaningful vitamin C content (~55 mg/100 g) and dietary fibre (~1.8 g/100 g). The primary clinical consideration is sodium content (~320 mg/100 g) attributable to soy sauce, which is relevant for individuals managing hypertension. The glycemic index is very low (~15), making it suitable for diabetic meal planning. Garlic contributes allicin and organosulfur compounds with potential cardioprotective and antimicrobial properties. The dish is naturally vegetarian, vegan, and dairy-free.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for stir-fried green pepper with garlic found in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central as a prepared dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FDC raw green bell pepper and garlic components with stir-frying adjustments. GI is estimated by analogy with non-starchy cooked vegetables from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium due to the estimation approach.
notes: 1. Food name corrected from input 'Stir Friedv Green Pepper with Garlic' (apparent typo 'Friedv') to canonical 'Stir-Fried Green Pepper with Garlic'. 2. Cuisine tags in input included 'Crab' and 'Lobster' which are inconsistent with a green pepper and garlic dish; these tags have been disregarded as likely data entry errors from the source platform. 3. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FDC components; a direct laboratory analysis or HPB Foodo entry would improve confidence to high. 4. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 as this is a plant-based dish containing no animal products; confirmed zero per USDA FDC data for plant foods. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 as green pepper and garlic contain no meaningful vitamin D; confirmed zero per USDA FDC. trans_fat_g is 0.0 as vegetable oil used in stir-frying contains negligible trans fats; confirmed zero per USDA FDC. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 as this is a fully plant-based dish; naturally zero. 5. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA FDC component data and are not -1 as reasonable estimates are available. 6. GI set to 15 by analogy with non-starchy cooked vegetables per University of Sydney GI Database conventions; GL per 150 g serving = 1.4. 7. Serving size of 150 g is an estimate for a typical restaurant side dish portion.
